---
title: "Electrical foundations"
description: "Safe observation, circuit analysis, measurement, and dynamic behavior."
page-role: part
part-id: foundations
---

Part I gives you the working habits that every later circuit depends on. You
learn to control the energy source, define electrical quantities and their
reference directions, predict circuit behavior, and measure without overlooking
the instrument's effect.

## The part question

> How can you make a physical circuit, its analysis, and its measurements
> describe the same behavior?

The first answers come from circuits made with sources, resistors, capacitors,
and inductors. These circuits are simple enough to inspect closely, but they
already contain the central engineering problems: loading, power, uncertainty,
stored energy, and limits on an approximation.

## Entry knowledge

No prior circuit theory is assumed. Basic algebra and scientific notation help.
[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) provides support for units,
prefixes, graphs, and equation manipulation. F06 draws on probability and
statistics from [M03](../appendices/m03-probability-numerical.qmd), while F07
introduces calculus and differential equations with support from
[M02](../appendices/m02-calculus-linear-differential.qmd).

Practical work starts with current-limited, extra-low-voltage systems and
locally approved procedures. The [preface](../preface.qmd) states the book-wide
safety and evidence boundaries.

## The learning arc

| Chapter | Main question | Capability |
|---|---|---|
| [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd) | How do you investigate a circuit without creating a hazard or corrupting the observation? | prepare the bench, control energy, estimate in SI, and keep a reproducible record |
| [F02](f02-charge-voltage-current.qmd) | What changes when a circuit transfers charge and energy? | define charge, voltage, and current with clear reference directions |
| [F03](f03-sources-loads-resistance.qmd) | Why does a source change when you connect a real load? | find source–load operating points and account for instrument loading |
| [F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd) | How do conservation laws determine node voltages and branch currents? | apply Kirchhoff's laws, superposition, and Thévenin/Norton equivalents |
| [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd) | Where does energy enter, accumulate, convert, and leave? | construct power and energy balances and check thermal limits |
| [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd) | What observation separates a circuit fault from a measurement error? | plan a measurement, state uncertainty, and isolate faults by hypothesis |
| [F07](f07-capacitors-inductors-transients.qmd) | How does stored energy shape a circuit's response to change? | predict first-order RC and RL transients |
| [F08](f08-sinusoids-impedance-frequency.qmd) | Why does a circuit respond differently at different frequencies? | use phasors, impedance, transfer functions, and Bode plots |

The sequence has four stages:

- F01–F02 establish safe observation and define the quantities you will use.
- F03–F05 develop static circuit analysis, operating points, power, and heat.
- F06 makes measurement and diagnostic reasoning systematic.
- F07–F08 extend the analysis into time and frequency.

## Prerequisite structure

The registry records direct prerequisites. In @fig-foundation-dependencies, the
same structure appears as arrows from each formal prerequisite to the chapter
that depends on it.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-foundation-dependencies
%%| fig-cap: "Direct prerequisites for Part I. Appendix nodes provide just-in-time mathematical support."
%%| fig-alt: "F01 leads to F02. F02 and M01 lead to F03. F03 leads to F04, F05, and F06, while M03 also leads to F06. F04, F05, and M02 lead to F07. F07 and M01 lead to F08."
%%| fig-width: 6.1
flowchart TB
  M01["M01<br/>algebra and units"]
  M02["M02<br/>calculus and dynamics"]
  M03["M03<br/>probability and statistics"]

  F01["F01<br/>safe practice"]
  F02["F02<br/>electrical quantities"]
  F03["F03<br/>operating points"]
  F04["F04<br/>networks"]
  F05["F05<br/>power and heat"]
  F06["F06<br/>measurement"]
  F07["F07<br/>transients"]
  F08["F08<br/>frequency response"]

  F01 --> F02
  F02 --> F03
  M01 --> F03
  F03 --> F04
  F03 --> F05
  F03 --> F06
  M03 --> F06
  F04 --> F07
  F05 --> F07
  M02 --> F07
  F07 --> F08
  M01 --> F08
```

Page order remains the clearest route for a new learner. If you already know
some of the material, the graph shows two useful paths:

- F01 → F02 → F03 → F06 develops trustworthy measurement early.
- F04 → F07 → F08 develops transient and frequency-domain analysis.

F05 and M02 also feed F07, so the second path becomes a complete dynamics route
only when those prerequisites are included.

## Practical work

The foundation labs use current-limited, extra-low-voltage hardware. Each lab
still needs defined apparatus, stop conditions, and local authorization.

| Lab | Aligned chapters | What you demonstrate |
|---|---|---|
| **L01** Bench safety and instrument qualification | F01, F06 | energize in stages and check whether the instruments suit the measurement |
| **L02** Source, load, resistance, and meter loading | F02, F03, F06 | measure a loaded circuit and account for the meter |
| **L03** Network conservation and equivalents | F04, F06 | compare Kirchhoff and equivalent-circuit predictions with hardware |
| **L04** Power, efficiency, and temperature rise | F05, F06 | compare a power balance with temperature measurements |
| **L05** RC/RL transients and frequency response | F07, F08, S01 | compare a time constant and frequency response with the bench |

Milestone **M1 — measure an energy-and-light system** combines F01–F06 with the
record-keeping practices in
[T03](../appendices/t03-reproducible-engineering.qmd). The
[practice and project spine](../roadmap.qmd#practice-and-project-spine) provides
the full integration map.

## Exit capability

After Part I, you should be able to:

- inspect and energize an approved low-energy circuit in stages;
- assign reference directions and interpret signed results;
- reduce a resistive network and predict its loaded operating point;
- construct a power budget and check component thermal limits;
- report a measurement with its uncertainty and isolate a fault by hypothesis;
- predict first-order transient and frequency responses within their stated
  operating ranges.

These skills support the active-device circuits in Part II, the timing and logic
of Parts III–IV, the signal and control analysis of Part V, and the verification
work in Parts VI–VII.

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity"}
All eight chapters are teaching drafts. They contain substantial exposition,
worked results, and exercises, but they remain subject to technical and
editorial review. The [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd#interpret-chapter-status)
explains the status levels.
:::
